  "goldenTruth": "POSITIVE|TRIMETHOPRIM|10 MG|SYMPTOMATIC HYPOGLYCEMIA"
}

- Phrase: The sentence to be processed by the large language model.
- goldenTruth: The expected output, using pipes as delimiters due to current limitations in structured JSON output. The name used in the SAP AI Launchpad for this value is "reference", however for consistency with the published paper and to demonstrate how to use different names in datasets we chose to use the more academically used term of "golden Truth".

The alignment between the "Phrase" (input) and "goldenTruth" (expected reference output) forms the basis for evaluating SAP AI Launchpad’s information extraction performance.

Methods / Technical Implementation

Models

The choice of models depends heavily on the intended use case, and as well on the cost of using the model. Currently it is common experience that generic models perform the best for question answering tasks, while tuned models are more appropriate for fact extraction. 

Generative AI Hub / Model Library

The Model Library allows to easily inspect which models are available, as well as their capabilities and properties. The Generative AI Hub is continuously updated with new LLMs having different capabilities, with Figure 2 below showing the status of the Model Library at the time of this writing.

Figure 2. SAP AI Launchpad Generative AI HUB Model Library

By clicking on a specific model one can get more information about that model. In Figure 3 below for example, are shown the details for  GPT 4.0. Among them, one can see that the model name is  “gpt-4.0” ( lowercase) and the version “latest” . These values must be used precisely later in the configuration file.

Figure 3. Details for the GPT-4o LLM in the SAP AI Launchpad Generative AI HUB Library

In this blog,  I am comparing some of the large language model available for processing and generating text:

  • Gemini 2.0 Flash
  • Mistral Large instruct
  • GPT 3.5-turbo
  • GPT 4.0

Prompt Engineering

Generative AI approaches are non deterministic and therefore require some level of experimentation before to achieve best results. The SAP AI Launchpad offers 2 different approaches to refine the prompt, namely through the Chat or through the Prompt Editor.

Generative AI Hub / Chat

At first one can try out the Chat, as shown below.

Figure 4. The Chat window in the SAP AI Launchpad

The first step is to  configure the Chat, this is done by clicking on “Configure”

Figure 5. The Configuration of the Chat tool in the SAP AI Launchpad

Here you can choose which model to use, and as well which parameters. The most important one is the “temperature”: setting it to the left end (0.0) makes the LLM delivering very conservative results, while setting to the right end (2.0) makes it more “creative”.

By typing or pasting the following text into the “Enter your message here” 

I am a pharmacovigilance expert and i need to know if a phrase contains evidence of a potential adverse drug event. For this reason i need you to support me by doing the following tasks, in the strict mode and without explanations. Do the following:
1. classify if a phrase as a whole could be indicative of a potential adverse drug event. if the first step is YES, then the output parameter "hasAE" must be set to "POSITIVE", otherwise to "NEGATIVE".
2.1 identify the drug name, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable "Drug", otherwise set it to X.
2.2 identify the amount of drug used, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable "Dose", otherwise set it to X.
2.3 identify the adverse event reported, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable "AdverseEvent", otherwise set it to X. 
The output must have the following structure:
hasAE|Drug|Dose|AdverseEvent.

Phrase: "An 83-year-old man receiving glipizide 10 mg bid developed symptomatic hypoglycemia within three days of adding trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (TMP/SMX) to his regimen

and then clicking the blue button results in an execution to be performed, and the results shown in Figure 6.

Figure 6. The result of execution of the SAP AI Launchpad

One  obtains the result that in this case is

POSITIVE|GLIPIZIDE|10 MG|SYMPTOMATIC HYPOGLYCEMIA

By clicking on the “Download” button the resulting output is a corresponding JSON representation as shown below.

[
  {
    "role": "user",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "text",
        "mimeType": "text/plain",
        "data": "I am a pharmacovigilance expert and i need to know if a phrase contains evidence of a potential adverse drug event. For this reason i need you to support me by doing the following tasks, in the strict mode and without explanations. Do the following: \n1. classify if a phrase as a whole could be indicative of a potential adverse drug event.\n2. if the first step is YES, then the output parameter \"hasAE\" must be set to \"POSITIVE\", otherwise to \"NEGATIVE\".\n2.1 identify the drug name, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"Drug\", otherwise set it to X.\n2.2 identify the amount of drug used, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"Dose\", otherwise set it to X\n2.3 identify the adverse event reported, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"AdverseEvent\", otherwise set it to X.\n3. The output must have the following structure:\nhasAE|Drug|Dose|AdverseEvent.\nPhrase: \"An 83-year-old man receiving glipizide 10 mg bid developed symptomatic hypoglycemia within three days of adding trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (TMP/SMX) to his regimen\"\n",
        "size": 1099
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "text",
        "mimeType": "text/plain",
        "data": "POSITIVE|GLIPIZIDE|10 MG|SYMPTOMATIC HYPOGLYCEMIA",
        "size": 49
      }
    ]
  }
]

The content.data element of the first section of the file could be then reused directly in the configuration runs (explained below) since it is properly formatted (new line characters are escaped, as well as quotes). 

The content.data element of the second section of the file contains the output.[

The disadvantage of using the Chat tool for prompt refinement is that (currently) there is no persistence, and therefore once a chat window is closed, everything is lost.

Generative AI Hub / Prompt Editor

Working with the Prompt Editor is similar to using the Chat , since one has to configure the same parameters, and one has to provide the Prompt, as shown in Figure 7.

Figure 7. Using the Prompt Editor

Please notice that if the variable “streaming response” is not ticked, also the intermediate steps will be returned, as shown in Figure 8.

Figure 8. The result of execution in the Prompt Editor.

In the Prompt Editor one can copy/paste the prompt into the “message blocks”, and one can as well save the prompt as template. This is very useful for additional changes and manual comparisons. Additionally, through using the prompt editor one introduce the concept of Variable Definitions and Variable Mapping. This is an essential step for the Evaluation later. Comparing the Prompts used in the chat vs in the prompt editor, one can see that the prompt editor does include the phrase to be analysed but instead uses a variable .

 

Chat

Prompt Editor

I am a pharmacovigilance expert and i need to know if a phrase contains evidence of a potential adverse drug event. For this reason i need you to support me by doing the following tasks, in the strict mode and without explanations. Do the following: \n1. classify if a phrase as a whole could be indicative of a potential adverse drug event.\n2. if the first step is YES, then the output parameter \"hasAE\" must be set to \"POSITIVE\", otherwise to \"NEGATIVE\".\n2.1 identify the drug name, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"Drug\", otherwise set it to X.\n2.2 identify the amount of drug used, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"Dose\", otherwise set it to X\n2.3 identify the adverse event reported, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"AdverseEvent\", otherwise set it to X.\n3. The output must have the following structure:\nhasAE|Drug|Dose|AdverseEvent.\nPhrase: \"An 83-year-old man receiving glipizide 10 mg bid developed symptomatic hypoglycemia within three days of adding trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (TMP/SMX) to his regimen\"\n"

I am a pharmacovigilance expert and i need to know if a phrase contains evidence of a potential adverse drug event. For this reason i need you to support me by doing the following tasks, in the strict mode and without explanations. Do the following: \n1. classify if a phrase as a whole could be indicative of a potential adverse drug event.\n2. if the first step is YES, then the output parameter \"hasAE\" must be set to \"POSITIVE\", otherwise to \"NEGATIVE\".\n2.1 identify the drug name, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"Drug\", otherwise set it to X.\n2.2 identify the amount of drug used, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"Dose\", otherwise set it to X\n2.3 identify the adverse event reported, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"AdverseEvent\", otherwise set it to X.\n3. The output must have the following structure:\nhasAE|Drug|Dose|AdverseEvent.
{{?Phrase}}

 

That is precisely the same approach that will be used for the large scale evaluation, where 300 hundreds of different phrases will have to be processed by the four different large language model and compared to the expected result.

Metrics

The precision, recall and F1 measures are the metric used in the reference paper (Gurulingappa, Rajput & Toldo, 2012) and here briefly explained:

  • True positive (TP) - These are the correct predictions made that are labeled as positive. You can input this and the below values in the confusion matrix calculator's first section.
  • False negative (FN) - These are the wrong predictions made that are labeled as negative.
  • False positive (FP) - These are the wrong predictions made that are labeled as positive.
  • True negative (TN) - These are the correct predictions made that are labeled as negative.

Using these four components, we can calculate various metrics to help us in analyzing the performance of AI strategy:

  • precision - Precision is the proportion of the correct predictions in the confusion matrix out of all positive predictions. precision = TP / (TP + FP)
  • recall - Recall is the proportion of correct predictions in the confusion matrix out of all positive classes. recall = TP / (TP + FN)
  • F1 score - F1 score allows you to compare low-precision models to high-recall models, or vice versa, by using the harmonic mean of precision and recall to punish extreme values. F1 score = (2 * precision * recall) / (precision + recall)

(Source: https://www.omnicalculator.com/statistics/confusion-matrix)

In information extraction (IE), the classical confusion matrix concepts of TP/FP/FN/TN must be adapted carefully, and the “True Negative” often loses relevance.

In practice:

  • TP = prediction matches golden truth (exact string match).
  • FP = system predicted something wrong (a wrong extraction).
  • FN = system missed the correct thing (no prediction or wrong prediction).
  • TN = usually not counted, because the “set of things not to extract” is unbounded.

This is why precision and recall in IE are usually defined as in the following formulas:

Example:

If your system:

  • Correctly extracts 80 entities (TP)
  • Extracts 100 entities total (so 20 are wrong → FP)
  • There were 90 true entities to extract (so 10 were missed → FN)

Then:

  • Precision = 80 / 100 = 0.80
  • Recall = 80 / 90 = 0.89
  • F1 = 2 * (0.80 * 0.89) / (0.80 + 0.89) ≈ 0.84

The SAP AI Launchpad provides a choice of several different metrics. These are shown in the step 3 of the configuration (see Figure 25).

BERT score is an automatic evaluation metric for text generation that computes a similarity score for each token in the candidate sentence with each token in the reference sentence.
It leverages the pre-trained contextual embeddings from BERT models and matches words in candidate and reference sentences by cosine similarity. Moreover, BERT score computes precision, recall, and F1 measure, which can be useful for evaluating different language generation tasks and therefore the appropriate choice for this work. However, one shall not be surprised in case of exact matches since due to the probabilistic approach, BERTscore calculation of precision and recall might slightly deviate from the mathematical one. Thus, precision and recall while theoretically range only between 0 and 1, when computed by BERTscore could exceed 1, perhaps at the 6th decimal digit. Practically, this difference should however have no impact on business use.

Orchestration Configuration

Quantitative Benchmarking AI strategies involves typically large number of “golden-truth” to be processeed, by different configurations consisting in different parameters and prompts. In the SAP AI terminology, this overall setup is described as an “orchestration”.

Each combination of Model, Prompt, Dataset, Grounding, Masking and Metrics that one wants to have executed must be specified in an Orchestration Configuration.

Given the design of the evaluation, in our case we are going to run 1 configuration per each model, while keeping all other parameters the same. The prompt, properly formatted, needs to be pasted into the template.content part of the JSON file.

Hereafter the content of the configuration file “gpt35-turbo.json”.

{
    "module_configurations": {
      "llm_module_config": {
        "model_name": "gpt-35-turbo",
        "model_params": {
          "max_tokens": 2000,
          "temperature": 0
        },
        "model_version": "latest"
      },
      "templating_module_config": {
        "template": [
          {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "I am a pharmacovigilance expert and i need to know if a phrase contains evidence of a potential adverse drug event. For this reason i need you to support me by doing the following tasks, in strict mode and without explanations. Do the following:\n1. Classify if a phrase as a whole could be indicative of a potential adverse drug event\n2. if the first step is YES, then the output parameter \"hasAE\" must be set to \"POSITIVE\", otherwise to \"NEGATIVE\". \n2.1 identify the drug name, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"Drug\", otherwise set it to X\n2.2. identify the amount of drug used, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"Dose\" , otherwise set it to X\n2.3 identify the adverse event reported, ensuring that it is part of the MEDDRA terminology, UPPERCASE it and use it as value for the output variable \"AdverseEvent\",otherwise set it to X.\n 3.The output must have the following structure:\nhasAE|Drug|Dose|AdverseEvent\n.{{?Phrase}}."
          }
        ],
        "defaults": {
        }
      }
    }
  }

SAP Object Store

The SAP Object Store plays a key role in the Evaluation. It is in the SAP Object Store that all the orchestration artifacts are stored and as well results of the different runs. The SAP AI Launchpad allows several different types of SAP Object Store to be used. In this blog I am using the AWS S3 implementation.

Preconditions

Following are the steps that needs to be done by the BTP Administrator of your account:

  • Have an entitlement for SAP Object Store 
  • Create an instance of the SAP Object Store

    Figure 9. The result of creating an Object Store instance.
  • Create  a service key of the SAP Object Store and hand that over to you as JSON file.

    Figure 10. The result of creating a Service Key from the  Object Store instance.

    Hereafter the content of that service key, that i used in this blog.
{
  "access_key_id": "AKIAU6GD22UIKT273FOW",
  "bucket": "hcp-cc9c52a2-5f62-4563-bcc2-fdfb0d582164",
  "secret_access_key": "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",
  "host": "s3-eu-central-1.amazonaws.com",
  "region": "eu-central-1",
  "uri": "s3://AKIAU6GD22UIKT273FOW:Z4Rtp%2B%2FX%2Fq5T%2FVzOhE1x%2Fv9CM4Da41AGo%2FfIMNjd@s3-eu-central-1.amazonaws.com/hcp-cc9c52a2-5f62-4563-bcc2-fdfb0d582164",
  "username": "hcp-s3-4755316a-f001-4ad4-9a1b-330767b77e0d"
}

Actions

1. Install aws client v2.
Once the artifacts are created ( the test dataset and the configurations for the runs that one wants to execute ) they need to be transferred into the Object Store. When using AWS S3 as object store implementation, then one needs to have the aws client locally installed and properly configured.
A local installation of the AWS client v2 is required, and something that is described in the corresponding documentation of AWS.

2. Configure the aws client
The configuration of the aws client requires information from the previously created service key.

Of all the parametrs of the service key the following are needed to configure the aws client:

keyvalues
access_key_id  AKIAU6GD22UIKT273FOW
buckethcp-cc9c52a2-5f62-4563-bcc2-fdfb0d582164
secret_access_key 4Rtp+/X/q5T/VzOhE1x/v9CM4Da41AGo/fIMNjd
region
eu-central-1

The configuration of aws client is performed as shown below:

%aws configure
AWS Access Key ID [None]:     AKIAU6GD22UIKT273FOW
AWS Secret Access Key [None]: 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
Default region name [None]:   eu-central-1
Default output format [None]:

By executing the above command, the files

.aws/config
.aws/credentials

will be created in your home directory and utilised by the aws commands for any subsequent operation.

These are plain text files and their content can be easily inspected

 .aws/config
[default]
region = eu-central-1

.aws/credentials
[default]
aws_access_key_id = AKIAU6GD22UIKT273FOW
aws_secret_access_key = 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

2. testing of the aws client

Detailed documentation of the commands available by the aws client is provided in the aws documentation.

The proper installation and configuration of the aws client can be simply verified by typing the following command:

aws s3api get-bucket-location --bucket hcp-cc9c52a2-5f62-4563-bcc2-fdfb0d582164

If the aws client has been properly configured, then it should return the value of the region parameter from the service key. 

{
"LocationConstraint": "eu-central-1"
}

Uploading of Artifacts

Once the artifacts ( configuration run, test dataset)  are created, one needs to push them to the appropriate folder structure to the S3 Object Store location as described in the documentation  Hereafter the local file structure as I createad for this explicit case, that included

  • one dataset, with 300 pairs of phrases and corresponding golden truth
  • four different run configurations, all having the same prompt and only changing the model used, since the onnline prompt management storage cannot be accessed by the execution runs.

Figure 11 shows the local file structure that i created for this blog.


Figure 11. The local file structure used in this blog.

To facilitate understanding of this blog,  a copy of the eval-data folder is accessible from the github repository created for this tutorial.

To push all of that data to AWS S3 one can simply perform the following command from within the eval-data folder:

aws s3 cp –recursive . s3://hcp-cc9c52a2-5f62-4563-bcc2-fdfb0d582164/ade-v2/eval/

That will generate the folder structure on AWS S3 precisely as locally, and the files will be appropriately copied over. One can verify the successful result by :

aws s3 ls --recursive s3://hcp-cc9c52a2-5f62-4563-bcc2-fdfb0d582164/ade-v2/eval/
2025-05-28 14:20:20       1388 ade-v2/eval/runs/gemini-2.0-flash.json
2025-05-28 14:20:20       1382 ade-v2/eval/runs/gpt-4o.json
2025-05-28 14:20:20       1384 ade-v2/eval/runs/gpt35turbo.json
2025-05-28 14:20:20       1405 ade-v2/eval/runs/mistral-large-instruct.json
2025-05-28 14:20:20      70662 ade-v2/eval/testdata/ade-v2-300-dataset.json

SAP AI Core Administration / Object Store Secrets

Due to the abstraction layer provided by platform, Object Store Secrets needs to be created in the SAP AI Core Administration. 

These secrets must be created in the resource group "default” , and one of them must have the variable "name" set equal to  “default”, as shown in the Figures below

In this example, the “ade-v2” object store secret is used to store the name and AWS credentials of the root folder where all input and output are stored. In our case, “ade-v2”.

The “default” object store secret is used to store the name and AWS credentials of the folder where the output for each run are going to be saved. In our case “output”.

To save or modify the secrets one has to enter in the “Secret” text area a properly formatted JSON object containing the corresponding fields from the Object Store Service Key and used for the  aws configuration too.

It is very practical to keep the following JSON object (make sure that the keys are CAPITAL as shown below, otherwise will not work ) easily available during the configuration creation process.

{
"AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID"     : "AKIAU6GD22UIKT273FOW",
"AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY" : "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"
}

 

ML Operations  / Other Artifacts

Due to the Object Store abstraction layer, that enables customers using different Object Store implementations, there is need of defining an additional “artifact” that provides the “root location” for all the artifacts used during the Evaluation run. This is a very important and required activity and hereafter i describe step by step how to do it.

Add new artifact is the starting activity.

 

The User Interface helps the creation of the artifact using a guided approach. After creating "add"  the step by step procedure is guided.

STEP 1 Select Scenario

Genai-evaluations is the option to be used as first step, as shown above.

STEP 2 General Information

The value used for the “Name” variable, is going to be used through the configuration and must be unique across all “other artifact”. It is a very important variable and therefore should be thought carefully. In my case due to the purpose of that variable, i named according to the location of the folder that contains both the run and the testdata.

In my case, this is /ade-v2/eval/, and therefore I called this variable ade-v2-eval.

STEP 3 URL
The value of the URL in step 3 is very critical:
  • The URL prefix must be ai://
  • The url must not finish with a “/”
  • The URL must be ai://fullpath-to-the-rootFolder
    where all the files are stored.

In our case

  
/ade-v2/eval  
  is the root folder since
/ade-v2/eval/runs
contains the configuration files
/ade-v2/eval/testdata
 contains the JSON formatted of the input data 

Therefore the value for the URL is:

ai://ade-v2/eval

STEP 4 Labels

No labels are required and therefore we can easily proceed to the last step.

STEP 5 Review

As for previous configuration activity, also for the “other artifact” creation it is possible to review all parameters before it being created.

The final outcome of that configuration can be seen in the “ML Operations / Other Artifacts”  page:

 

The ade-v2-eval will be used later as the first step in the creation of the Configuration in the Evaluation page.

Experiments and Results

Configuration and Execution

At this point everything is ready to create an overall configuration orchestration, and then have execute the 1200 runs.

To create an Evaluation one simply click on the “create” button.

That initiates a 4 step guided approach:

STEP 1 Select the Test artifact.

This step requires 4 variables to be specified

Test Artifact
this is the previously generated artifact that has the location of the rootFolder.
in our case “ade-v2-eval” that points to “ade-v2/eval”

Runs
this is simply the folder name , relative to the root, where the run configuration files have been saved.
In our case the root is “ade-v2/eval”, the folder is “ade-v2/eval/runs” and therefore here goes only “runs”.
We want to have all those 1200 runs executed, therefore we only specify the folder name. Otherwise one could have selected individual files only.

Dataset Format
The dataset can be provided in either JSON or CVS, in our case we choose JSON,

Dataset Location,

related  to the rootFolder: testdata/ade-v2-300-dataset.json
since it is in “ade-v2/eval/testdata” folder.

 STEP 2 Select the Metric

As previously mentioned, the SAP AI Launchpad offers several metrics to calculate the quality of the results and compare them to your reference  golden standard. One or more metrics can be selected by simply clicking on the user interface.

Each metric might require mapping variables between your dataset and the internal configuration of the metric. This is achieved in the “Additional Configurations” step as shown below. 

STEP 3 Additional Configuration

As described in the SAP AI Core documentation  documentation, BERTscore requires a variable “reference” mapped to the “The ground truth reference string provided by the user”. This implies, that BERTscore can only be applied to a single “string” and not to a JSON object with sub-component. Ideally, we would like to have aBERTscore for each of the components of the ADE: Drug, Dose, Adverse Event, hasAE, however that is currently not yet supported. Therefore we combine all of those outputs in a single string, pipe separated so that at postprocessing (outside the SAP AI Launchpad) one could compute those individual components too. While we are using only the BERTscore in our run, to facilitate usage of multiple metrics one can use the target prefix all_metrics, and that is what is shown below.
In the reference dataset, we called "goldenTruth" the reference value. Here we therefore have to specify that

all_metrics.reference needs to be mapped to the data.goldenTruth

If in the input dataset instead of using "goldenTruth" we would have used the string "reference", then no mapping would be required.

STEP 4 Review and Create Execution Job

Once all parameters are provided, a final page allows to review them and eventually navigate back and modify them accordingly.

By clicking on the “create” button, the following 2 things will happen:

  1. A new configuration is created and displayed in the ML Operations / Configurations tab
    Here you can see a list of many configurations that I created in the past.
    Unfortunately at the moment it is not possible to delete configurations, but perhaps this will change in future versions.  
    By clicking on a “ML Operations / Configurations” item, one can see all the details specific for that configuration.

     

    The button “Create Execution” is very useful, for example when modifying the content of the runs folder or of the testDataset, and then wanting to resubmit the same configuration without modifying the  parameters.
    However, if the configuration creation has been successful, the configuration will have been already automatically submitted for execution
  2. A new Execution is created in the ML Operations / Executions tab.
    That page can be inspected to follow the progress of the execution and / or identify changes required due to errors in the configuration or in the data .


    You can monitor the status of the execution by clicking on “ML Operations / Executions“ and then selecting the appropriate one.  All executions that one has performed can be seen in the “ML Operations / Execution” page, however, in that page one can also delete individual executions (and is good practice to keep a clean environment).

This was the longest part of this blog series, in the next one we are going to see the output that has been generated, and therefore answer the initial question: are the LLM going to potentially revolutionise Pharmacovigilance ?

entry.title: Benchmarking Generative AI for Drug Safety Using SAP AI Launchpad - Part 1 of 3
entry.content:
Rethinking Pharmacovigilance with Generative AI

This blog post is the first in a three-part series that I authored to showcase the capabilities and maturity of the SAP AI Launchpad. It illustrates how the platform supports quantitative evaluation of diverse AI strategies. While pharmacovigilance is not currently within the scope of any SAP AI product offering, I chose this domain due to the complexity of the task, my personal expertise in assessing outcome quality, and the availability of high-quality public datasets.

All datasets, configurations, results, and technical resources can be found here:
👉GitHub Repository

⚠️Important Note:
This study is based on the internal May/June 2025 release of SAP AI Launchpad. While minor changes may appear in the general availability (GA) version, the overall workflow, challenges, and key insights will remain applicable.

Disclaimer: Pharmacovigilance is a highly regulated area governed by GxP requirements. This blog does not address GxP compliance or the suitability of SAP AI Launchpad for GxP-regulated applications, nor does it make recommendations in this regard.

PART 1- Rethinking Drug Safety with Generative AI and SAP AI Launchpad

Key Highlights:

  • Historical context: Hippocrates, Paracelsus, and the evolution of pharmacovigilance
  • The rise of Adverse Drug Reactions (ADRs)
  • Motivation for AI in drug safety
  • SAP AI Launchpad as a business-user-ready, zero-code solution

Theme: The problem space and the promise of AI in pharmacovigilance

Target audience: Healthcare professionals, AI enthusiasts, SAP users

Goal: Set the stage for why this work matters and how SAP AI Launchpad fits into the future of pharmacovigilance.

Abbreviations used:

  • LLM: Large Language Models
  • ADRs: Adverse Drug Reactions (singular form: ADR) 

Primum Non Nocere

Hippocrates of Kos (circa 460–370 BCE), often regarded as the father of modern medicine, is credited with the maxim "First, do no harm" (primum non nocere), which underpins medical ethics to this day. This foundational principle dictates that every medical intervention should enhance, or at the very least, not diminish the patient’s quality of life. Even in antiquity, it was recognized that any therapeutic action could entail risks, potentially exacerbating a patient’s condition.

Sola Dosis Facit Venenum

Approximately 1,500 years after Hippocrates, Philippus Aureolus Theophrastus Bombastus von Hohenheim—better known as Paracelsus (1493–1541 CE)—famously asserted in his work "Third Defense": "Solely the dose determines that a thing is not a poison" (Sola dosis facit venenum). Paracelsus’s insight laid the foundation for the field of toxicology (Michaleas SN et al.,2021). Today, the determination of the LD50 (lethal dose for 50% of a tested animal population) remains a key step in the safety assessment of new compounds prior to their approval for human use (Morris-Schaffer, K & McCoy, MJ., 2021).

Adverse Drug Reactions 

Subsequent centuries of medical practice revealed that even minimal doses of drugs could precipitate severe adverse outcomes, such as anaphylactic reactions. This recognition compelled the implementation of more rigorous preclinical and clinical assessments prior to drug approval. Regulatory authorities were established to oversee the approval and withdrawal of pharmaceuticals, supported by a standardized definition of Adverse Drug Reactions (ADRs) (Kommu S et al.,2024)

A response to a drug which is noxious and unintended, and which occurs at doses normally used for prophylaxis, diagnosis, or therapy of disease or the modification of physiologic function.

While clinical trials involve relatively small cohorts, the evaluation of ADRs is an ongoing process that extends well beyond initial market authorization.

The societal impact of ADRs has grown markedly over recent years. In 2012, it was estimated that 6% of hospitalizations involved severe ADRs; by 2018, that figure had risen to nearly 10%. In 2012, ADR-related deaths in the United States were estimated at 100,000 annually; by 2025, this number is projected to have tripled. While this increase may partially reflect improved reporting and an aging population, the absolute numbers remain alarming. ADRs are now the fourth leading cause of death in the United States, representing a major obstacle to drug development and a primary reason for drug withdrawals, with considerable societal and financial consequences.

Background & Problem Statement

Revolution in Pharmacovigilance

According to harmonized international guidelines—such as the International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use (ICH E2C R2, ICH, 2012)—the Marketing Authorization Holder is obliged to continuously assess all relevant safety information for their products. This information may originate from clinical trials, spontaneous reports by healthcare professionals, scientific literature, or even social media. The goal is an ongoing reassessment of benefit–risk profiles throughout the lifecycle of a medicinal product.

Until the late 1990s, pharmacovigilance primarily involved manually querying scientific literature databases and laboriously reviewing reports. However, with the advent of the World Wide Web and the democratization of content publishing, it became increasingly evident that manual methods were inadequate to tackle the exponential growth in information sources.

Modern pharmacovigilance now faces an overwhelming and ever-expanding array of data sources—comprising millions of case reports, scientific publications, regulatory filings, and real-time social media feeds. These data streams are often multilingual and highly unstructured, making comprehensive signal detection and timely intervention a formidable challenge. Manual methods and even traditional machine learning techniques, while valuable, are increasingly unable to keep pace with the scale, complexity, and evolving nature of adverse event data.

By 2013, automated methods for identifying potential ADRs in free text had been developed and demonstrated high accuracy (Gurulingappa et al., 2013). These approaches typically relied on manually annotated corpora (Gurulingappa et al., 2012) and traditional machine learning algorithms. More recently, the introduction of generative AI models has been identified as a transformative advance for drug safety and pharmacovigilance (Mishra HP and Gupta R,2025).

SAP AI Launchpad, with its code-free interface and flexible integration of large language models (LLMs), represents a potential paradigm shift. By empowering professionals and business users—regardless of their programming expertise—to configure, evaluate, and deploy advanced AI models, SAP AI Launchpad offers the promise of dramatically accelerating signal detection, streamlining regulatory compliance, and reducing the human and economic toll of undetected safety signals.

Automatic Detection of Potential Adverse Drug Reactions

For this study, I employ the benchmark dataset created by Gurulingappa et al. (2012), as detailed in their publication and available at Hugging Face. This gold standard corpus comprises systematically annotated MEDLINE case reports, capturing mentions of drugs, adverse effects, dosages, and the relationships between them. Stringent annotation protocols have ensured the reliability of the data for information extraction research. The creation process and quality evaluation are thoroughly described in the original paper. Notably, this corpus was the first of its kind to address adverse drug events with such scientific rigor and has become a central resource for subsequent natural language processing  competitions in this domain.

A specific aim of this work is to evaluate how SAP AI Launchpad compares to the previously published results that utilized relational semantic extraction technologies—such as support vector machines—for ADR detection (Gurulingappa, Matteen-Rajput & Toldo, 2012 ). Demonstrating that SAP AI Launchpad can meet or exceed these benchmarks would have significant implications, not only for enabling scalable, cost-effective pharmacovigilance within the pharmaceutical industry, but also for contributing to improved patient safety outcomes throughout healthcare systems worldwide.

Solution Overview: Leveraging SAP AI Launchpad for Pharmacovigilance

By applying SAP AI Launchpad to public ADR datasets, I demonstrate that sophisticated pharmacovigilance tasks can be performed:

  • Without programming knowledge

  • Without specialized hardware

  • Using an enterprise-grade, SaaS-based LLM solution

This unlocks advanced text mining and signal detection capabilities for healthcare professionals and regulatory teams—at scale, reliably, and efficiently.

entry.title: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 25.6
entry.content:
Hello Community,

I am happy to announce that a new release of the Mobile Development Kit(MDK) is available for all Mobile Services customers and can be downloaded on the SAP Software Center and will soon be available on community Download page as well.

The SAP Mobile Services Client has also been updated to 25.6.0 both in the Google Play Store and in Apple App Store.

SAP Mobile Development Kit enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / VSCode extension) and run it natively on Mobile (Android & iOS).  

Below enhancements are available only on iOS and Android platforms:

  • New UI enhancements
    •  Support Grouping in Object Table Control
    • Support Body Elements in Card Collection Control
    • Donut Chart Support
    • Support Customizing Placeholder in Form Cell List Picker Control
    • Bottom Sheet Support for Popover Menu (Android Only)
    • Support Top Caption in Form Cell Segmented Control (iOS Only)
  • API and Syntax enhancements
    • Support Lock and Wipe Settings in the MDK Client
    • Support Device Compliance in MDK Client
    • Expose Generate Content API from Gemini LLM
    • Simplify Application Launcher and Splash Screen Generation in MDK Branded Client
    • Support for getCell in DataTableRowProxy

Support Grouping in Object Table Control 

MDK now supports grouping items in an Object Table section, making it easier to get an overview of data on large list screens.

//excerpt of a page 
"Grouping": {
    "GroupingProperties": [
        "CategoryName"
    ],
    "Header": {
        "Items": [{
            "Title": "Category: {CategoryName} ",
            "Styles": {
                "Title": "DataTableGroupHeaderItem",
                "BackgroundColor": "sectionBgColor"
            }
        }]
    }
},
"_Type": "Section.Type.ObjectTable",
….

Grouping in Object Table control

For more information, refer to the Grouping documentation and its API Reference.

Support Body Elements in Card Collection Control

The Card Collection control provides brief, related pieces of information and serves as an entry point, teaser, or preview for more detailed content. By tapping on a card, users can select it and navigate to a dedicated page with expanded information.

We have now introduced Body Elements in the Collection control. The card body is the central part of the card, used to display additional information alongside the content shown in the card header. It is a separate container that can hold various components, with a flexible height to accommodate a reasonable number of UI elements.

Following contents can currently displayed in Card Body Elements currently supports:

  • Text
  • Label Bar
  • Space
  • Separator
//excerpt of a page
   "Body": {
        "_Type": "Card.Type.Body",
        "OnPress": "/MDK2504/Rules/Card/ShowToastMessage.js",
        "Contents": [
            {
                "_Type": "CardBodyContent.Type.Text",
                "Text": "The card body is the central part of the card that is used to display additional information alongside the content shown in the card header.",
                "NumberOfLines": 5
            },
            {
                "_Type": "CardBodyContent.Type.LabelBar",
                "Layout": {
                    "LayoutType": "Vertical"
                },
                "ItemSeparator": true,
                "Items": "/MDK2504/Rules/Card/CardStaticContentLabelBarItems.js"
            },
            {
                "_Type": "CardBodyContent.Type.Space",
                "NumberOfSpacings": 3
            },
            {
                "_Type": "CardBodyContent.Type.Separator",
                "_Style": "card-label-2"
            }
        ]
    },

Body Elements in Card Collection Control

For more information, refer to th Card Collection documentation and its API Reference.

Donut Chart Support  

You can now use the Donut Chart type to visualize data in a new way that emphasizes various aspects of the dataset. Donut charts are ideal for displaying proportions of categorical data in a part-to-whole analysis. They take a single dataset and represent the ratios in a circular format.

Support for the Donut Chart type is available in the Analytic Card Collection, Chart Content Section, and Object Header Chart view UI controls.

Donut Chart in iOS

Donut Chart in Android

For more information, see these documentation on Donut chart support in Analytic Card Collection, Chart Content Section, and Object Header.

Support Customizing Placeholder in Form Cell List Picker Control

We have introduced a new property, Placeholder, for the List Picker Form Cell control to customize the placeholder text when no value is selected. This property provides contextual cues to guide business users in making their selections.

 
//excerpt of a page 
{
    "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.ListPicker",
    "_Name": "FormCellListPicker0",
    "PickerPrompt": "Please select one single item",
    "PlaceHolder": "Select Item",
    "IsEditable": true,
    "PickerItems": [
        "Alpha",
        "Bravo",
        --
    ]
}​
 
 

For more information, see this  documentation.

Bottom Sheet Support for Popover Menu (Android Only)

A new property, IsBottomSheet, has been introduced for the Popver Menu Action.

  • When set to true, the popover is displayed as a bottom sheet.
  • When set to false or not defined, the popover is displayed as a dialog.
{
    "PopoverItems": [{
            "Icon": "sap-icon://synchronize",
            "OnPress": "/MDK2504/Actions/Service/SyncStartedMessage.action",
            "Title": "Sync Changes"
        }
    ],
    "_Type": "Action.Type.PopoverMenu",
    "IsBottomSheet": true
}

 

For more information, see this  documentation.  

Support Top Caption in Form Cell Segmented Control (iOS Only)

Prior to this release, MDK displayed segmented Form Cell controls on iOS as single-line buttons, which  would dynamically switch to a stacked layout. With this release, we are introducing a new property for the Segmented Control: CaptionPosition. When this property is set to Top, the control will always use stacked layout.

{
    "CaptionPosition": "Top", //"Adaptive"
    "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SegmentedControl",
    "_Name": "FormCellSegmentedControl2",
    "Caption": "Top Caption",
    "ApportionsSegmentWidthsByContent": false,
    "Segments": [
        "Low",
        "Medium",
        "High"
    ]
}

For more information, see this  documentation.  

Support Lock and Wipe Settings in the MDK Client

We are adding support for Locking and Wiping settings on MDK client. This new feature will significantly improve security and protect business users' sensitive information, helping safeguard them against unauthorized access or misuse of their devices.

  • Lock (users): Lock users on the specified device and force them to re-authenticate to continue using the app.
  • Wipe (device registration) : Lock the user and clear all app data (including any local changes) on the device. User has to re-onboard to continue using the app.

 Administrator can lock users and wipe device registrations through the following options:

  • Initiate Locking or Wiping actions directly from the mobile services Admin UI using toggle buttons.

 

  • Set up offline locking/wiping policies that automatically lock or wipe the device when the configured number of days expires. Number of days are based on how long the app is offline (without connecting to server).

For more information, see this  documentation.  

Support Device Compliance in MDK Client

MDK introduces Device Compliance Detection, a security feature that allows app developers to perform various security checks to determine whether a device has been compromised.

Administrators must enable the Device Compliance Detection policies in the mobile services Admin UI to ensure compliance reports are sent to Mobile Services.  

When the Compliance Policy is enabled in the Mobile Services Admin UI:

  • Detection is automatically performed during app login and each time the app is restarted.
  • The compliance status for each registration will be visible in the Mobile Services Admin UI.

For more information, see this  documentation.  

To check the status of your device, we have added a new Client API isDeviceCompliant(). 

  • If it returns true, the device is compliant. 
  • If it returns false, the device is non-compliant or has been compromised.
var isRootDevices = clientAPI.isDeviceCompliant();

Support Generate Content API from Gemini LLM

In MDK’s previous release 24.11, we introduced support for leveraging the Chat Completion LLM API.

In this release, we are now introducing a new MDK Action called Generate Content, which connects to SAP AI Core and explicitly supports integration with Gemini Generate Content API, enabling seamless content generation. This API allows developers to create dynamic, structured content for articles, reports, and multimedia outputs, leveraging advanced AI models like Gemini 1.0 Pro and Gemini 1.5 Pro.

Instead of calling an LLM API provided directly by an LLM service provider, SAP AI Core offers a unified way to access LLM APIs from the most popular LLM service platforms. SAP AI Core manages the AI model and ensures scalable deployment in enterprise applications, maintaining compliance and security.

 The Generate Content LLM API can address various scenarios, including:

  • Text Input: Submit text prompts to the API to receive AI-generated responses.
  • Input: Enhance the interaction by including images, allowing for a multimodal conversational experience.
  • Function Calling: Enable the AI model to invoke specific functions or execute commands based on the conversation’s context.
{
  "_Type": "Action.Type.AICore.Generate.Content",
  "Target": {
    "Service": "/MDKDemoApp/Services/OpenAI.service",
    "Path": "/models/gemini-1.5-pro:generateContent"
  },
  "Properties": {
    "Contents": []
  },
  "ShowActivityIndicator": true,
  "ActivityIndicatorText": "Processing ...",
  "ActionResult": {
    "_Name": "GenerateContent"
  },
  "OnFailure": "/MDKDemoApp/Actions/Failure.action"
}​

For more information, see this  documentation.  

Simplify Application Launcher and Splash Screen Generation in MDK Branded Client

Prior to this release, the effort required to customize Application Launcher icon and Splash screen is non-trivial. A developer has to generate these icons and screens using different ways and different sizes for different platforms as documented here.

We have simplified and unified generating these application launcher icon and Splash screen for developers to provide these images under a single location.

A new property, AppImages, has been introduced in MDKProject.json , where you can mention the file details in the given properties.

"AppImages": {
  "Launcher": {
    "Logo": "launcher",
    "Background": "android_launcher_background", // Android only
  },
  "Splash": {
    "Logo": "splash_logo",
    "Background": "background", // iOS only
    "LogoBackground": "android_splash_background", // Android only
  }
}

All those files should be placed under yourMDKApp.mdkproject>App_Resources>MDK>Images

Please note you can still continue previous way of customization as mentioned in our documentation. However, in order to use this new way, we highly suggest to remove any customization or override files added for the launcher and splash screens in the App_Resources and App_Resources_Merge folders for both iOS and Android.

For more information, see this  documentation.  

Support for getCell in DataTableRowProxy

With this release, we are introducing getCell feature in DataTableRowProxy, enabling you to retrieve a DataTableCellProxy object using the column index of the target cell. This new functionality will seamlessly let you access and interact with specific cells within a table row.

let cellParent = context.getParent();
let columnIndex = context.getColumnIndex();
let getCell = cellParent.getCell(columnIndex).getValue();

New to MDK development?
Follow  these tutorials  to get started and learn more about Mobile development kit!
I am looking forward to your feedback/comments.

entry.title: AI Agents: Simple Guide for Enterprise Architects
entry.content:
Understanding Future Automation for Business

The words "AI agent" were very popular in 2024. From what I see, it is a big trend for 2025. For Enterprise Architects, our job is to check new technologies and put them into our IT systems. This helps our companies change to digital ways. This guide explains AI agents in a simple way. It talks about how they work and what we, as architects, need to think about. This is based on my own work experience.

What is an AI Agent? New Digital Helper

An AI agent is an autonomous computer program. It perceives its environment, processes information, makes decisions, and then acts autonomously to achieve predefined goals. Unlike AI chatbots, which just reply to what users say, AI agents can make their own decisions.

This helps them automate complex tasks in companies, like customer service, data analysis, or even helping with coding. They can either fully automate tasks or augment human capabilities in daily work. For instance, I've observed AI agents in online retail. They recommend products, answer questions, and process orders. If they need more information, they ask the user themselves.

AI Agent vs. AI Chatbot: What is Different?

Many people use "AI agent" and "AI chatbot" as the same thing. They are alike in some ways. Both use Natural Language Processing (NLP) to understand language. Often, Large Language Models (LLMs) power them. And both can connect to other systems. But AI agents are different in important ways. Here is how you can tell them apart:

Feature

AI Chatbots

AI Agents

Purpose

Talks to users for small tasks.

Automates big tasks, makes choices, and does many steps.

Decision-Making

Reacts to user input, follows rules.

Thinks, plans, and acts by itself, no direct user input.

Form

Usually a chat box on a website or app.

Can work in the background or be a chat interface.

This difference is key for choosing the right solution. A sales chatbot answers questions. But a sales AI agent can guess which customers will buy more and send them personalized messages. It's about acting first, not just reacting.

How AI Agents Work: They See, Think, Do, and Learn

To really use AI agents, we must understand how they operate. When I see them work, they always follow a clear process. It's like a loop: they see, they process, they decide, and then they act. From a technical view, it is very smart:

  • Getting Information (Perceiving): Agents first get information. This can be from sensors (like cameras in self-driving cars), from users (like voice commands), or from company data (databases, logs, documents). The Perception Module takes this raw information and makes it ready for the agent to understand.
  • Thinking & Deciding (Processing & Decision-Making): After getting data, the agent analyzes it to decide what to do. They use NLP for text and check data signals. Decisions can be based on simple rules, or on Machine Learning (ML) predictions from past data. Some use Reinforcement Learning (RL), learning by trying and getting feedback. Advanced agents use Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). This helps them find correct information from big databases before answering, which makes answers right and reduces errors. Also, Prompt Engineering guides the LLM to think step-by-step and pick the right tools.
  • Taking Action (Performing Tasks): When the agent decides, its Action Module does the task. This can be giving a text answer, controlling a device, running business processes (like orders in ERP), using APIs to connect to other systems, or managing many tasks at once. Agents can complete whole workflows.

Learning & Getting Better (Improvement): What makes AI agents smart is that they learn from what they do. They watch what happens after their actions and get better over time. This happens with feedback. Like a system that recommends things learning what you like, AI agents keep improving.

The AI Agent's Inside Parts: How It's Built (Technical View)

For Enterprise Architects like us, knowing how AI agents are built inside is important. It's how the smart software works with the computer systems. There are four main parts, often made with special frameworks:

  • Profiling Module: This part defines who the agent is and what its main goal is. This is set using a "system prompt" for the LLM. It also handles the first information from the environment.
  • Memory Module: This is key for the agent to remember and use past information. It has two types:
    • Short-Term Memory: What the agent is thinking about right now, like a recent conversation.
    • Long-Term Memory: Old knowledge stored outside, like in vector databases, used with RAG to find information quickly.
  • Planning Module: This is the agent's "brain" for making strategies. It takes big goals and breaks them into smaller steps. It also chooses the right tools. LLMs can do this planning, or older AI methods can be used.
  • Action Module: This part makes the agent's decisions happen. It translates choices into commands for tools, APIs, or other systems. For robots, these are physical parts. For software, there are links to other programs.

So, Agent = Architecture + Agent Program. The architecture is the system it runs on, and the program tells it what to do.

  • Key Frameworks: To build these agents, we use frameworks. These help us build faster and better:
    • LangChain: A popular framework for LLM agents, memory, and tools.
    • CrewAI: Good for making many AI agents work together.
    • Microsoft Semantic Kernel: Helps add AI to existing apps.
    • AutoGen (Microsoft): For multi-agent conversations.

 

Different Kinds of AI Agents

To pick the right AI agent for a business problem, we need to know different kinds exist. An agent sees, decides, and acts by itself for specific goals. Here are common types for us:

  • How They Act:
    • Reactive Agents: Act fast based on what they see now. Like a smart light turning on when it's dark.
    • Proactive Agents: Plan ahead for future goals. Like an agent planning a sales strategy for next month.
  • Where They Work:
    • Fixed Environments: Rules don't change.
    • Dynamic Environments: Always changing, agents must adapt fast.
  • How Many Agents:
    • Single-Agent Systems: One agent works alone.
    • Multi-Agent Systems (MAS): Many agents work together to reach a big goal. They can be same or different, work together or against each other.
  • Rational Agents: They always try to make the best choice.
  • Other Types (based on how they are built): Simple Reflex, Model-Based Reflex, Goal-Based, Utility-Based, Learning, Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI), Logic-Based, Hierarchical Agents. Each for different kinds of tasks.

What AI Agents Can Do: Real Uses in Companies

The best thing about AI agents, for me, is how flexible they are. I've seen them make many different processes much smoother across all kinds of businesses. For Enterprise Architects, knowing these uses helps us find good places to start:

  • Software & IT Work: Agents can be like co-pilots for coders, helping with debugging and writing code. They can also automate IT support, check system logs, and send alerts. This really helps engineers.
  • Customer Service & Sales: More than just chatbots, agents improve how we talk to customers. They give personal product suggestions, find good sales leads, and talk to customers first.
  • Knowledge Management: This is very helpful. Agents can find information fast from company documents, help centers, and solve problems, saving lots of time.
  • Workflow Automation: This is where AI agents show their true power. They manage many steps across different systems. Like buying things for the company, helping new employees, managing deliveries, or changing prices online. It's smart, connected automation.
  • Virtual Assistants: They are like super helpers. They can plan schedules, do research, analyze data, write emails, or plan trips by themselves.
  • Special Industries: They are making a big impact in areas like finance (trading, finding fraud), healthcare (patient help), and transport (traffic).

Why AI Agents Are Good for Your Company: The Benefits

Bringing AI agents into your company offers many advantages. They are flexible and can make decisions independently, speeding up tasks. They're also versatile, so an agent built for one job can often handle others without being rebuilt.

AI agents are available 24/7, which is great for global businesses, and can save money by automating tasks that people used to do. They can complete entire workflows, from connecting with other systems to getting approvals, streamlining your processes. They connect easily with your existing tools, making your IT systems work better together. Plus, they learn quickly, so you'll see faster results and a quicker return on your investment. Finally, they're more accurate and compliant, strictly following company rules and regulations, which is key for safety and governance.

However, there are risks to consider. Compliance is a big one; agents need to follow laws like GDPR, and mistakes can lead to legal and financial issues. Sometimes AI can make up facts (hallucinations), but this can be managed by giving agents correct information and having people check their work. It can also be hard to understand why an agent made a certain decision, especially in regulated industries. To fix this, you should record their thought process and get human approval for important choices. Lastly, AI agents aren't a "set and forget" solution; they need ongoing monitoring and updates as your business changes.

The Way Forward: AI Agents and the Future of ERP

The shift from simple data retrieval (RAG) to full AI Agents is a game-changer for businesses. Picture your old ERP system, once just a record keeper, becoming a super-smart, active hub. It won't just list tasks; it'll complete, improve, and adapt them using clever AI.

Imagine your AI-powered ERP system:

  • Proactive Supply Chain: It could spot problems before they happen, adjust inventory automatically, and even negotiate with suppliers for better deals, all while following your company's rules.

  • Automated Finances: It could intelligently check bills, detect fraud, fix payment issues, and predict money trends in real-time, keeping your finances healthy and accurate.

  • Enhanced Customer & Employee Experiences: AI agents could directly handle complex customer queries, take care of routine HR tasks, and offer personalized help. This frees up your team to focus on more important, creative work.

This is the future: your ERP as the central brain of your company, making operations faster, stronger, and much smarter. It empowers your people to innovate and tackle complex challenges.

So, as Enterprise Architects, how will you integrate AI Agents into your company? How do you envision them transforming your ERP systems and overall business operations? Let's discuss and share our insights.

Learn More: Deeper into AI Agents

For Enterprise Architects and technical people who want to learn more about AI agents & SAP Business AI, here are some helpful places I have found:

  • How They Work & How They're Built:
    • IBM: What Are AI Agents? - Good overview of how agents work, with focus on tools. [ Link ]
    • Azilen Technologies: AI Agent Architecture: Explained with Real Examples - Shows how agents are built, step by step. [ Link ]
  • Tools & Libraries for Building Agents:
    • LangChain Documentation: A very popular tool for making LLM agents. [ Search "LangChain agents" on their official docs (Exact URL may vary by version). ]
    • Prompt Engineering Guide: LLM Agents - Learn about making agents with LLMs and how to give them good instructions. [ Link ]
    • Botpress: Top AI Agent Frameworks - Compares different tools like Botpress, LangChain, CrewAI. [ Link ]
    • ControlFlow: A Python tool to build AI workflows, good for control. [ Link ]
  • Research & New Ideas:
    • GitHub: A collection of AI Agents papers (Updated often) - Many academic papers about AI agents. [ Link ]
    • MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy: New studies about agentic AI - Recent research from MIT. [ Link ]
  • SAP Business AI | Product Resources & News: https://www.sap.com/canada/products/artificial-intelligence/resources.html
  • Unlock the Power of SAP Business AI Through Learning and Certification: https://www.sap.com/documents/2025/05/7e1cdd1b-067f-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html
  • Artificial Intelligence | SAP Learning: https://learning.sap.com/artificial-intelligence
  • Exploring the SAP Business AI Portfolio - SAP Learning: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/exploring-the-sap-business-ai-portfolio-1
